boundaries.definition: Rules or limits that show what is acceptable and what is not.

define.definition: To describe clearly what something means or is.
define.example: Define yourself and not the child when setting rules.

metaphorical.definition: Not real or physical; used as a comparison to explain something.
metaphorical.example: A boundary is a metaphorical line in the sand.

calmly.definition: In a quiet and peaceful way, without anger.
calmly.example: State a boundary calmly but firmly.

firmly.definition: In a strong, sure way that shows you mean it.
firmly.example: She spoke firmly so her child knew she was serious.

grabbing.example: You might end up grabbing back your keys and yelling.

yelling.example: Yelling at a child can frighten them.

longed-for.definition: Wanted very much for a long time.
longed-for.example: A much-longed-for child arrived after many difficulties.

miscarriages.definition: When a pregnancy ends too early and the baby does not survive.
miscarriages.example: They had several miscarriages before their child was born.

IVF.definition: A medical process to help people have a baby when they cannot naturally.
IVF.example: After IVF, they finally had their child.

tragic.definition: Very sad, often involving death or great suffering.
tragic.example: In some tragic circumstances, parents lose a child.

blinded.definition: Unable to see or think clearly because of strong feelings.
blinded.example: They were so blinded by love they forgot to set limits.

miracle.definition: Something wonderful and unexpected that seems impossible.
miracle.example: The baby felt like a happy miracle.

revere.definition: To respect and admire someone very deeply.
revere.example: Some parents revere their child like a god.

progeny.example: They revere their progeny like a god.

omnificent.example: Believing you are omnificent goes beyond healthy self-esteem.

self-delusion.definition: Believing something about yourself that is not true.
self-delusion.example: Without limits, a child may go from confidence into self-delusion.

structure.definition: An organized system or framework that supports something.
structure.example: Boundaries provide structure to our lives.

habit.definition: Something you do regularly, often without thinking.
habit.example: Defining yourself is a good habit to get into.

nagging.definition: Repeatedly telling someone to do something in an annoying way.
nagging.example: I'm tired of listening to my voice nagging about teeth.

effective.definition: Working well and getting the result you want.
effective.example: Make your boundaries effective by meaning what you say.

threats.definition: Warnings that something bad will happen if rules are broken.

shatter.definition: To break into many pieces; to destroy completely.
shatter.example: A scary threat can shatter a child's ability to think.

relent.definition: To give in after first refusing.
relent.example: Do not relent and give the keys back after setting a boundary.

spoiling.definition: Giving a child everything they want so they become badly behaved.
spoiling.example: Respecting a child is not spoiling them.

tease.definition: To annoy or bother an animal or person in a playful or unkind way.
tease.example: I cannot let you tease the dog.

disapprove.definition: To think that something is wrong or bad.
disapprove.example: Your tone will teach them you disapprove of the activity.

embodied.definition: Felt or learned through the body, not just the mind.
embodied.example: They will learn on an embodied level what is appropriate.

weapon.definition: Something used to hurt or fight.
weapon.example: The child was using the toy keyboard as a weapon.

inappropriate.definition: Not right or suitable for the situation.
inappropriate.example: If the inappropriate behaviour continues, take the toy away.

consistent.definition: Always doing things the same way; dependable.
consistent.example: Be consistent so your child knows you mean what you say.

removal.definition: The act of taking something or someone away.
removal.example: Physical removal teaches your child to take you seriously.

instructed.example: They do as instructed because they know you mean it.

empathize.definition: To understand and share how someone else feels.
empathize.example: Listen and empathize with their feelings.

manipulating.definition: Controlling someone in a clever, often unfair, way.
manipulating.example: Make sure you are relating, not manipulating.

boisterous.definition: Very noisy, active, and full of energy.
boisterous.example: The children were getting more and more boisterous.

hysteria.example: They were on the edge of hysteria.

trooped.example: In they all trooped when she said it was time to go inside.

framing.definition: Presenting or saying something in a particular way.
framing.example: Framing boundaries with less negativity helps.

negativity.definition: An attitude that focuses on bad things.
negativity.example: Use as little negativity as possible when setting rules.

rupture.definition: A break or serious damage in a relationship.
rupture.example: She learned to repair the rupture with her toddler.

reward.definition: Something good you receive because of something you did.
reward.example: Her hug was my reward for good parenting.

environment.definition: The world around you; the conditions you live in.
environment.example: Boundaries help children consider the environment and others.

objective.definition: Based on facts, not personal feelings.
objective.example: Don't pretend to be objective when your feelings are involved.

screen time.definition: Time spent watching TV, using a phone, or playing on a computer.
screen time.example: Many parents limit their child's screen time.

sane.example: Listening to your feelings is key to staying sane.

fact tennis.definition: An argument where each side hits back with reasons instead of sharing feelings.
fact tennis.example: Pretending to be objective invites a game of fact tennis.

temper.example: Losing your temper can traumatize your child.

traumatize.definition: To cause lasting emotional damage from a very upsetting experience.
traumatize.example: Losing your temper can traumatize a child.

fuse.definition: In 'blow a fuse': to suddenly become very angry.
fuse.example: The limit is when you blow a fuse.

memes.definition: Funny pictures or videos shared widely on the internet.
memes.example: Two hours of YouTube memes was too much for her.

cartoons.definition: Drawings or animated shows, often funny, usually for children.
cartoons.example: He watched cartoons for two hours.

worthy.definition: Deserving respect or having a good purpose.
worthy.example: Don't hide your real feelings behind worthy reasons.

pornography.definition: Pictures or videos showing sexual content.
pornography.example: It is important to talk to children about pornography.

petty.definition: Small and not important; mean in a small way.
petty.example: Children may resent rules that seem petty.

autistic.definition: Having autism, a condition that affects how a person communicates and interacts.
autistic.example: Their eldest child was autistic and needed routine.

resent.definition: To feel angry or bitter because something seems unfair.
resent.example: Children may resent unfair or inflexible rules.

resentment.definition: A lasting feeling of anger about something unfair.
resentment.example: Storing up resentment is storing up trouble.

thumb.definition: In 'rule of thumb': a general guide that is usually true.
thumb.example: The rule of thumb is to define yourself, not the child.

absorbed.definition: Very interested in and focused on something.
absorbed.example: The child was absorbed in playing music.

selfish.definition: Thinking only about yourself and not others.
selfish.example: It felt like a risk to own my selfishness.

playground.definition: An outdoor area where children play.
playground.example: She told her daughter she was bored at the playground.

leftovers.definition: Food that was not eaten and is saved for later.
leftovers.example: We can eat the leftovers instead of cooking.

negotiate.definition: To talk and try to reach an agreement that works for everyone.
negotiate.example: It is easier to negotiate when everyone shares their feelings.

grumpy.definition: In a bad mood; easily annoyed.
grumpy.example: I'll get grumpy if I don't have lunch.

inclination.definition: A feeling that makes you want to do something.
inclination.example: A respected child has less inclination to act out.

observing.definition: Watching carefully to learn or understand something.
observing.example: With young children, you need to listen by observing.

speech.definition: The ability to talk and use words.
speech.example: Paul has speech and language difficulties.

autism.definition: A condition that affects how a person communicates and relates to others.
autism.example: His difficulties are probably related to autism.

diagnosis.definition: A doctor's decision about what condition someone has.
diagnosis.example: They have had no formal diagnosis for his condition.

war zone.definition: A place where there is a lot of fighting and conflict.
war zone.example: The house sometimes felt like a war zone.

perspective.definition: A way of seeing or thinking about something.
perspective.example: We started to understand life from his perspective.

patience.definition: The ability to wait calmly without getting annoyed.

nudge.definition: A gentle push to encourage someone in a direction.
nudge.example: We learned when to nudge him further and when to back off.

puzzle.definition: To try hard to understand something confusing.
puzzle.example: We didn't have to puzzle her out as much.

considerate.definition: Thinking about other people's feelings and needs.
considerate.example: As we became more considerate, she did too.

cornerstones.definition: The most important and basic parts of something.
cornerstones.example: The four cornerstones of appropriate behaviour are essential.
